title: Riding Round The Lines
content:
혻혻혻혻Dust and smoke against the sunrise out where grim disaster lurks
혻혻혻혻And a broken sky-line looming like unfinished railway works,
혻혻혻혻And a trot, trot, trot and canter down inside the belt of mines:
혻혻혻혻It is General Greybeard Shrapnel who is riding round his lines.

혻혻혻혻And the scarecrows from the trenches, haggard eyes and hollow cheeks,
혻혻혻혻War-stained uniforms and ragged that have not been off for weeks;
혻혻혻혻They salute him and they cheer him and they watch his face for signs;
혻혻혻혻Ah! they try to read old Greybeard while he占퐏 riding round the lines.

혻혻혻혻There占퐏 a crack, crack, crack and rattle; there占퐏 a thud and there占퐏 a crash;
혻혻혻혻In the battery over yonder there is something gone to smash,
혻혻혻혻Then a hush and sudden movement, and its meaning he divines,
혻혻혻혻And he patches up a blunder while he占퐏 riding round his lines.

혻혻혻혻Pushing this position forward, bringing that position back,
혻혻혻혻While his officers, with orders, ride like hell down hell占퐏 own track;
혻혻혻혻Making hay, and to what purpose?, while his sun of winter shines,
혻혻혻혻But his work is just beginning when he占퐏 ridden round his lines.

혻혻혻혻There are fifty thousand rifles and a hundred batteries
혻혻혻혻All a-playing battle music, with his fingers on the keys,
혻혻혻혻And if for an hour, exhausted, on his camp bed he reclines,
혻혻혻혻In his mind he still is riding, he is riding round his lines.

혻혻혻혻He占퐏 the brains of fifty thousand, blundering at their country占퐏 call;
혻혻혻혻He占퐏 the one hope of his nation, and the loneliest man of all;
혻혻혻혻He is flesh and blood and human, though he never shews the signs:
혻혻혻혻He is General Greybeard Shrapnel who is fixing up his lines.

혻혻혻혻It is thankless work and weary, and, for all his neighbour knows,
혻혻혻혻He may sometimes feel as if he doesn占퐐 half care how it goes;
혻혻혻혻But for all that can be gathered from his eyes of steely blue
혻혻혻혻He might be a great contractor who has some big job to do.

혻혻혻혻There占퐏 the son who died in action, it may be a week ago;
혻혻혻혻There占퐏 the wife and other troubles that most men have got to know, 
혻혻혻혻(And we占퐇l say the grey-haired mother underneath the porch of vines):
혻혻혻혻Does he ever think of these things while he占퐏 riding round his lines?

혻혻혻혻He is bossed by bitter boobies who can never understand;
혻혻혻혻He is hampered by the asses and the robbers of the land,
혻혻혻혻And I feel inclined to wonder what his own opinions are
혻혻혻혻Of the Government, the country, of the war and of the Czar.

혻혻혻혻He占퐏 the same when he占퐏 advancing, he占퐏 the same in grim retreat;
혻혻혻혻For he wears one mask in triumph and the same mask in defeat;
혻혻혻혻Of the brave he is the bravest, he is strongest of the strong:
혻혻혻혻General Greybeard Shrapnel never shows that anything is wrong.

혻혻혻혻But we each and all are lonely, and we have our work to do;
혻혻혻혻We must fight for wife and children or our country and our screw
혻혻혻혻In the everlasting struggle to the end that fate destines;
혻혻혻혻In the war that men call living we are riding round our lines.

혻혻혻혻I ride round my last defences, where the bitter jibes are flung,
혻혻혻혻I am patching up the blunders that I made when I was young,
혻혻혻혻And I may be digging pitfalls and I may be laying mines;
혻혻혻혻For I sometimes feel like Shrapnel while I占퐉 riding round my lines.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
